name: Big Digital Energy, Inc.

profile.description: Big Digital Energy, Inc. (formerly Mawson Infrastructure Group Inc., renamed April 2026) is a technology company that designs, develops, and operates digital infrastructure platforms. The company offers artificial intelligence and high-performance computing colocation, digital asset (Bitcoin) mining, and technology infrastructure services. Based in Midland, Pennsylvania, the company operates data center facilities and is pivoting from pure crypto mining toward AI/HPC colocation. The company has 33 employees and faces Nasdaq delisting risk after stockholders' equity fell below required thresholds.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (1/3): BGDE has attempted a strategic pivot from pure Bitcoin mining to AI/HPC colocation, signing a 75MW colocation deal with Endeavor Group in 2026 [2]. However, execution has been poor — revenue declined from $84.4M in 2022 to $39.8M in 2025, and Q1 2026 revenue collapsed to $4.8M [1]. The rebrand from Mawson Infrastructure to Big Digital Energy in April 2026 represents a cosmetic change rather than a proven business transformation. Previous digital colocation revenue surged 222% in one period but has since deteriorated significantly. AI Deployment (1/2): The company markets AI and HPC colocation services, positioning its data center infrastructure as AI-enabling capacity [2]. The Endeavor Group agreement targets AI/HPC workloads, suggesting early-stage AI infrastructure revenue. However, no specific AI product revenue or measurable AI-driven cost savings have been quantified in earnings disclosures. Market Share (1/2): BGDE is a marginal participant in the competitive data center colocation market, competing against far larger operators like Equinix, Digital Realty, and purpose-built AI infrastructure companies. The 75MW Endeavor deal provides some capacity but represents a tiny fraction of the AI/HPC colocation market [2]. Pipeline (1/2): The Endeavor Group strategic agreement provides some pipeline visibility for AI/HPC colocation revenue, and new management has articulated a strategy to identify assets in the AI and HPC markets [2]. However, specifics on revenue timelines and capacity buildout remain vague. R&D Intensity (0/1): No evidence of meaningful R&D investment; the company is an infrastructure operator rather than a technology developer.

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (1/4): BGDE's stated purpose is to 'design, build, and operate next-generation digital infrastructure platforms' [3]. This is functional and generic — it describes what the company does but articulates no deeper purpose or vision for why it exists beyond serving the digital infrastructure market. The rebrand to Big Digital Energy suggests identity searching rather than mission clarity. Community Impact (0/2): No evidence of charitable programs, community investment, or foundation activity. The company's sustainability page from the Mawson era highlighted 92.7% renewable energy usage and a Net Zero 2030 goal, but these are operational targets rather than community impact programs [3]. As a 33-person company facing going concern risk, community giving is understandably not a priority. Employee Retention (0/2): With only 33 employees and recent complete leadership reconstitution — new CEO Phil Stanley, new Executive Chairman Joshua Kilgore, and new COO Cody Smith — the organization has experienced significant management turnover [2]. No retention data, tenure data, or employee sentiment information is available. The rapid leadership changes suggest organizational instability. Insider Buying (1/2): New management team installed in 2026 has taken leadership stakes, with Joshua Kilgore serving as Executive Chairman [2]. However, no specific insider buying data with dollar amounts was found, and the new leadership team's financial commitment remains unclear.

infrastructure.label: WEAK
infrastructure.analysis: BGDE's financial metrics are uniformly distressed. D/E of 677.8% is far above the 300% floor, reflecting an overleveraged balance sheet with $29.4M in debt against minimal equity [4]. ROE is deeply negative at -15.7%, indicating the company is destroying shareholder capital. Revenue collapsed 65.1% YoY, with Q1 2026 revenue of only $4.8M versus $13.8M in Q1 2025 — a 65% quarterly decline [1]. Profit margin is -73.9%, reflecting severe operating losses. No forward P/E or PEG ratio is available because analysts do not provide earnings estimates for the company. FCF is negative at -$25.5M. Most critically, management disclosed 'substantial doubt about continuing as a going concern' in Q1 2026, and Nasdaq issued a delisting determination in April 2026 after stockholders' equity fell below the $2.5M threshold [1]. Every available financial metric scores at the floor (0.0). Liquidity floor cleared: 597K avg vol x $10.67 = $6.4M daily [4].

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (4/9): BGDE occupies an ambiguous position in the AI ecosystem — the company is attempting to pivot from Bitcoin mining to AI/HPC colocation, which would make it a beneficiary of AI infrastructure demand rather than a victim of AI disruption [2]. However, the core Bitcoin mining business that generated most historical revenue faces indirect AI risk as AI workloads compete for the same energy and data center resources, potentially driving up costs for crypto miners. The company's data center infrastructure could theoretically serve AI customers, but BGDE competes against far better-capitalized colocation providers (Equinix, Digital Realty, CoreWeave) with superior scale, connectivity, and financial stability. The going concern risk and Nasdaq delisting threat make the AI pivot execution highly uncertain [1]. AI Opportunity (1/1): BGDE has signed a 75MW colocation agreement with Endeavor Group specifically targeting AI/HPC workloads, and the company explicitly markets AI and HPC services as a core business line [2]. While revenue from AI-specific services has not been separately quantified, the infrastructure is being positioned for AI compute customers.

infinite_game.summary: Big Digital Energy exhibits predominantly finite game characteristics. The company has undergone multiple identity shifts — from Mawson Infrastructure to Big Digital Energy — chasing market trends from crypto mining to AI/HPC without establishing a durable competitive position in either. Complete leadership reconstitution, Nasdaq delisting risk, and going concern disclosures suggest a company playing to survive this quarter rather than playing to keep playing. There is no evidence of a Just Cause, organizational trust, or strategic patience.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (0/3): BGDE has no articulated Just Cause. The company's identity has shifted from Mawson Infrastructure Group (crypto mining) to Big Digital Energy (AI/HPC colocation) within a year, suggesting strategic opportunism rather than enduring purpose [2]. The mission of operating 'next-generation digital infrastructure platforms' is a description of activities, not a vision of a future state worth sacrificing for. The rebrand itself signals that leadership is searching for identity rather than advancing a cause. Sinek would view this as a classic finite player — chasing whatever market narrative generates the most investor interest in a given quarter.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (0/2): No evidence of courageous long-term investment decisions. Leadership changes have been reactive — the entire board and management team were reconstituted in 2026, with new CEO Phil Stanley, Executive Chairman Joshua Kilgore, and COO Cody Smith installed amid financial distress [2]. The company's strategic decisions appear driven by survival needs (rebranding, pivoting to AI/HPC, seeking Nasdaq compliance) rather than conviction about a long-term vision. Revenue declined 65% YoY while management focused on governance restructuring and name changes rather than operational turnaround [1].

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): BGDE's organizational environment shows signs of low trust and instability. The company has only 33 employees and experienced complete leadership reconstitution in 2026, suggesting significant internal disruption [2]. No Glassdoor reviews, employee sentiment data, or retention metrics are available. The going concern disclosure and Nasdaq delisting notice create existential uncertainty for employees, which Sinek's framework identifies as antithetical to building Trusting Teams — people cannot feel safe when the organization's survival is in doubt [1].

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): BGDE has shown willingness to transform its business model, pivoting from pure Bitcoin mining to AI/HPC colocation with the Endeavor Group agreement and company rebrand [2]. The 75MW colocation deal represents an attempt to redirect existing data center infrastructure toward higher-value AI workloads. However, Sinek distinguishes between existential flexibility driven by a Just Cause (Apple abandoning Mac for iPhone) and desperation-driven pivots. BGDE's pivot appears motivated by financial survival — revenue collapsed, the company faces delisting, and crypto mining became unprofitable — rather than advancing a greater purpose. This is adaptation by necessity, not strategic courage.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): No evidence of an infinite competitive mindset. BGDE's competitive positioning focuses on claiming space in the AI/HPC colocation market without acknowledging the vast advantages of worthy rivals like Equinix, Digital Realty, and CoreWeave [2]. Management communications emphasize market opportunity rather than learning from competitors' strengths. The company's competitive language is focused on market positioning rather than self-improvement.

investment_thesis: Big Digital Energy is attempting a high-risk pivot from declining Bitcoin mining operations to AI/HPC data center colocation, signing a 75MW agreement with Endeavor Group in 2026 [2]. The stock trades at a $59M market cap, and if the AI/HPC pivot gains traction, the company's existing data center infrastructure could be re-valued at significantly higher multiples. The new management team under CEO Phil Stanley and Executive Chairman Joshua Kilgore brings fresh strategic direction, and the broader AI infrastructure buildout creates genuine demand for colocation capacity. Bitcoin mining operations previously achieved 92.7% renewable energy usage, which could differentiate BGDE's AI colocation offering [3].
thesis_continued: The bear case is severe and dominates. Management disclosed 'substantial doubt about continuing as a going concern' in Q1 2026, and Nasdaq issued a delisting determination after stockholders' equity fell below $2.5M [1]. Revenue collapsed 65.1% YoY to $39.8M in 2025, with Q1 2026 revenue of only $4.8M — a catastrophic 65% quarterly decline from Q1 2025. The company burned $17.1M cash in Q1 2026 while generating only $4.8M revenue, creating an unsustainable cash drain [1]. D/E of 677.8% reflects a dangerously overleveraged balance sheet with $29.4M in debt against minimal equity and only $2.4M in cash. FCF is deeply negative at -$25.5M. The complete leadership reconstitution in 2026 — with no management team member having tenure beyond one year — creates execution uncertainty during a critical pivot. BGDE competes for AI/HPC colocation business against operators with vastly superior scale, financial resources, and customer relationships [4].
key_catalysts: Successful execution of the 75MW Endeavor Group AI/HPC colocation agreement could establish recurring infrastructure revenue and validate the pivot from Bitcoin mining to higher-margin data center services [2]
New management team under CEO Phil Stanley and Executive Chairman Joshua Kilgore may stabilize the capital structure and unlock value through operational restructuring and asset identification [2]

AI infrastructure demand tailwind — the massive buildout of AI compute capacity creates demand for colocation services, and BGDE's existing data center infrastructure with renewable energy sourcing could attract ESG-conscious AI customers [3]
key_risks: Going concern risk — management disclosed substantial doubt about the company's ability to continue as a going concern in Q1 2026, with $17.1M quarterly cash burn against only $2.4M in cash and $29.4M in debt [1]

Nasdaq delisting determination — stockholders' equity fell below the $2.5M minimum threshold, and failure to regain compliance through the hearing process could result in delisting to OTC markets, further reducing liquidity and institutional access [1]

Revenue collapse and competitive disadvantage — revenue declined 65% YoY with Q1 2026 at $4.8M, and the company must compete for AI/HPC colocation customers against Equinix, Digital Realty, and CoreWeave, which have vastly greater scale, capital, and established customer relationships [1][4]
sources: Big Digital Energy Q1 2026 10-Q Filing — $4.8M revenue, $0.6M net income, $17.1M cash burn, going concern disclosure, Nasdaq delisting determination, May 2026
